title: Needle-felted whale
subtitle: A compact blue-grey humpback whale figure with white belly and fluke detail
excerpt: Needle-felt a smooth humpback whale in blue-grey merino with a white underbelly, the characteristic bumpy rostrum knobs, and broad horizontal flukes. The whale body is an elongated teardrop compacted from a single roving length; the flukes are made separately and attached. A steady first figure project that introduces the method of making and attaching appendages.
sourceNotes: Needle-felted whale figures are a common contemporary craft piece. The elongated-teardrop construction with separate flukes is the standard form used in workshops.

glossaryTerms.definition: The horizontal tail fin of a whale, made in needle felting as two flat lobes attached to the narrow tail end of the body. The horizontal orientation (unlike a fish's vertical tail) distinguishes cetacean anatomy.
The rounded tubercles on the top of a humpback whale's head and along the leading edges of its flippers. In needle felting, each knob is a small pre-compacted dot of roving needled onto the surface.

content.text: The body of the whale is an elongated teardrop: wider at the head end and tapering to a narrow tail. The
are made as two flat oval lobes and attached at the tail end, angled slightly downward. The
are tiny pre-compacted dots worked onto the head top. The finished whale is about 12 cm long.

attrs.title: Needle safety
attrs.body: Stab straight in and out at 90 degrees. The needle must enter foam, not a hard surface, on every stroke.

content.text: Take 18 g of blue-grey roving and compact it into an elongated shape approximately 12 cm long, with one end (the head) rounder and broader and the other end (the tail) narrower. Work the head end first to establish the size, then ease the tail end into a taper by concentrating strokes there. The belly should be slightly flatter than the back.

Lay a veil of white roving along the flat underside of the body, from chin to the base of the tail. Needle from the edge of the white area inward with the size 40 fine needle, blending the edge softly into the blue-grey. The belly should cover about one-third of the body circumference.
Step 3: Make and attach the flukes

Needle 4 g of blue-grey roving into two flat oval lobes, each about 3 cm long and 1.5 cm wide. These are the two fluke lobes. Leave one end of each lobe slightly loose (not fully compacted). Hold both lobes at the narrow tail end of the body, fanning outward horizontally with the loose ends overlapping at the centre. Needle through both loose ends into the tail to attach, then add a thin layer of blue-grey roving over the join and needle it smooth.

Pre-compact tiny pinches of blue-grey roving into dots about 3 mm across on the mat. Place them in a row along the top of the head and needle them down with the fine needle. They should sit as small raised bumps. Three to five knobs are sufficient for a realistic impression.

items.problem: The body looks too stocky rather than streamlined
items.cause: Head and tail ends compacted at the same rate without emphasising the taper
items.fix: Work the tail end with more concentrated strokes to compact it further and elongate the taper.

items.problem: The flukes stick up vertically rather than lying flat horizontally
items.cause: Lobes angled upward when attached instead of outward
items.fix: Attach with the lobes lying flat on the mat and needle while they are pressed horizontal.

items.problem: The white belly creeps up the sides
items.cause: Too much white roving laid, or strokes worked over the blue-grey edge
items.fix: Use less white roving as a veil. Needle from the white inward only; never needle from blue-grey toward white.
